# Same-Day Focaccia

High-hydration pan focaccia, mixed at breakfast and baked for dinner. No
starter required — instant yeast — so it's the bread for weeks when the
sourdough starter is asleep in the fridge.

## Formula

- 500g bread flour
- 400g water (80% hydration)
- 10g salt
- 4g instant yeast
- 40g olive oil for the pan and the top, plus more for finishing
- Flaky salt, rosemary

## Morning: mix and fold

Stir flour, water, salt, and yeast into a shaggy mass — no kneading. Cover.
Three sets of folds in the bowl over the first 90 minutes, then let it rise
at room temperature until tripled and bubbly, 4–6 hours. This dough is far
wetter than a boule dough; resist adding flour, it's supposed to pour.

## Afternoon: pan proof

Slick a 9x13 metal pan generously with olive oil. Pour the dough in, fold
it over itself once, flip seam-down, and let it relax until it fills the
pan on its own — about an hour. Then oil your fingers and dimple firmly all
the way to the pan bottom; the dimples hold pools of oil and keep the crumb
open. Top with flaky salt and rosemary. Proof another 45–60 minutes until
jiggly.

## Bake

230°C, middle rack, 22–26 minutes until the top is deep gold and the
underside (lift a corner with a spatula) is fried crisp from the pan oil.
Out of the pan onto a rack immediately — left in the pan it steams and the
bottom goes soft.

## Troubleshooting

Dense focaccia is almost always an under-risen morning bulk — give the
first rise the full tripling, not a timer. Pale bottom means too little
pan oil or a glass pan; metal conducts the heat that fries the base. If
the dimples spring back instantly the dough needed more bench rest before
dimpling.
